content:
I've created Secure TCP module that I'd like to get to the official Rust-For-Linux tree.

Sources are available at https://github.com/MiesSuomesta/STCP/tree/main/kernel/OOT/linux

How should I progress ?
What is the intended user of STCP?
IoT devices for example
Does this module expose an API used by userspace?
The API is same as the proper TCP.. It is drop in replacement for TCP connections
Simple by changing the protocol number used in socket creation => 253 (default now) .. and one gets secure TCP ...
the socket/protocol hooks will take care of the secure handshaking etc. and userspace code otherwise can be untouched..

There is no "Rust-For-Linux" tree as such, because Rust for Linux stopped being a fork of linux a long time ago. We have a path for core rust code into upstream linux, but I don't think that is what you are looking for. To get this code merged into upstream Linux, you would have to use the same channels that C code is using.

However, I am not exactly sure what path regular C code would take for this module. I would assume something like networking?

Does this STCP feature exist in the kernel today, or is it a new network protocol family? If it is a new protocol, there is a discussion to be had with the people who maintain the user space networking syscalls.

The STCP is completely new module.. it uses C & Rust, the beef is in Rust, and C only delegates operations to Rust. It is meant to be TCP connection replacement, so that user just needs to create socket with IPPROTO_STCP instead of IPPROTO_TCP ...

Ok, cool. I don't know too much about that area, but I think you would want to start a discussion with the network people. The first question to answer here is not a Rust one, it is whether or not this is something the networking people want in the kernel.
I would imagine that they are going to ask why this cannot be implemented by a user space library over UDP instead.
Yeah, I would suggest first seeking consensus on the netdev mailing list about merging the new STCP protocol. Usually, it would be much harder than implementing a new protocol.

I've implemented the protovol in userspace also, but the module would enable anything to be secured with protocol number change => no need to implement all the userspace stuff per application. The protocol uses TCP as transport layer, and hooks handle the elliptic handshake + AES en/decryption ...

Lauri Jakku said:

I've implemented the protovol in userspace also, but the module would enable anything to be secured with protocol number change => no need to implement all the userspace stuff per application. The protocol uses TCP as transport layer, and hooks handle the elliptic handshake + AES en/decryption ...

Right, I understand. But you probably want to query how the networking community feels about this, as Tomonori suggest.

I have no idea if they would be open to something like this, but in general it is my understanding that people are reluctant to add features to the kernel if it is possible to do the same thing in user space without performance loss. For this particular feature, you would still have to modify user space programs, to use the different protocol ID. Since you are now recompiling user pace, you might as well link in a library that intercepts all socket operations and implements the feature in user space. But again, you need to ask the networking community. This is just my guess as to what they would say.

Andreas Hindborg kirjoitti:

Lauri Jakku said:

I've implemented the protovol in userspace also, but the module would enable anything to be secured with protocol number change => no need to implement all the userspace stuff per application. The protocol uses TCP as transport layer, and hooks handle the elliptic handshake + AES en/decryption ...

Right, I understand. But you probably want to query how the networking community feels about this, as Tomonori suggest.

I have no idea if they would be open to something like this, but in general it is my understanding that people are reluctant to add features to the kernel if it is possible to do the same thing in user space without performance loss. For this particular feature, you would still have to modify user space programs, to use the different protocol ID. Since you are now recompiling user pace, you might as well link in a library that intercepts all socket operations and implements the feature in user space. But again, you need to ask the networking community. This is just my guess as to what they would say.

OK, I'll seek the mainainer(s) of network code and start discussion with them.

I don't see how this can be fully transparent to userspace. Userspace would have to tell the kernel things like encryption keys or allowed certificates, right? If you allow any certificate, then it would be just as insecure as unencrypted connections against an active attacker. They can just present their own certificate. Also how does this compare against something like wireguard? That doesn't require any changes to individual applications at all, just needs an admin to configure a network interface and then tunels any network protocol for the specified ip address ranges.
And TLS is a thing too and used by most encrypted network traffic. You can run anything over it, not just HTTP.

bjorn3 kirjoitti:

I don't see how this can be fully transparent to userspace. Userspace would have to tell the kernel things like encryption keys or allowed certificates, right? If you allow any certificate, then it would be just as insecure as unencrypted connections against an active attacker. They can just present their own certificate. Also how does this compare against something like wireguard? That doesn't require any changes to individual applications at all, just needs an admin to configure a network interface and then tunels any network protocol for the specified ip address ranges.

Nope, user does NOT to have any knowledge of encryption details: Everything is handled dynamically. The module uses random private & publickeys and uses the kernel crypto for generating shared key. There is nothing that user have to do except change the socket's protocol number from IPPROTO_TCP => IPPROTO_STCP.

Everything is made dynamic, no need to know nothing from encryption, that is the beauty of SCP module.

Documentation of the handshake & everything else: https://github.com/MiesSuomesta/STCP/tree/main/kernel/OOT/linux/docs

The protocol can replace every TCP connection making them very secure with minimal effort.

There is nothing in your handshake that verifies the identity of peer. You can prevent eavesdropping with this but not MITM.

Regardless, if you want to get your design accepted, the important thing is to get networking communities as a whole to accept it (not just kernel networking people, but more general one). The choice of language of the implementation is the least important part.
Encryption without authentication has very limited use, if any.

Andreas Hindborg kirjoitti:

Encryption without authentication has very limited use, if any.

Well, it could enhance old plaintext TCP by adding security layer (stop plain text transmissions). I don't see reason not to merge the code to kernel..

It does no change anything existing, but gives extra security layer that is very easy to use.

This would be good security upgade for all TCP connections.

I don't see reason not to merge the code to kernel..

I think you will probably need to show users will want it -- I would recommend writing a good RFC for the networking maintainers/community/lists, explaining the use cases and comparing it with alternative solutions (including the usual userspace ones).

By the way, this should probably be moved to our #Networking, do you mind if I move it?

Hi,

Thanks for feedback, I'll make RFC for the communities..

And: yes you can move this to proper place :)

--Lauri
You're welcome!
This topic was moved here from #Modules > STCP (TCP connection replacement module) by Miguel Ojeda.

Hi,

I Created RFC at https://github.com/MiesSuomesta/STCP/blob/main/kernel/OOT/linux/RFC.md ..

There is Director level interest of the protocol from Nordic Semiconductor and such. => There is
need for the module.

Where I should publish the RFC ?

--Lauri J
Making security review as part of the future work will look quite bad for something like this.

Onur Özkan kirjoitti:

Making security review as part of the future work will look quite bad for something like this.

I'm creating the review after New Year .. The review is quite short, cause the protocol's transport is ordinary TCP.

Lauri Jakku kirjoitti:

Onur Özkan kirjoitti:

Making security review as part of the future work will look quite bad for something like this.

I'm creating the review after New Year .. The review is quite short, cause the protocol's transport is ordinary TCP.

https://github.com/MiesSuomesta/STCP/blob/main/kernel/OOT/linux/docs/SECURITY_AND_HANDSHAKE_ANALYSIS.md .. there you go :)

Where I should publish the RFC ?

You should send it to Linux's networking community (e.g. mailing list, maintainers, etc.), i.e. this Zulip is not really the right place to decide on particular features for Linux -- it is the subsystems that decide on that.
This sort of feature would probably require an IETF RFC

Gary Guo kirjoitti:

This sort of feature would probably require an IETF RFC

Ok, Ill create proper IETF RFC

Lauri Jakku kirjoitti:

Gary Guo kirjoitti:

This sort of feature would probably require an IETF RFC

Ok, Ill create proper IETF RFC

I've just now contacted IETF organization with proper IETF RFC ( https://github.com/MiesSuomesta/STCP/blob/main/kernel/OOT/linux/docs/IETF%20RFC.md )
